narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, you've shared your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, LSAT score of 34, upper-middle family income background, and plans to attend full-time, along with your school decile rankings from Year 1 and Year 3 both at the second decile. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 99.2% chance that you would fail the bar exam—such a high risk led directly to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes applicants likely to pass and succeed in their legal careers. The most influential factor pushing the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, well below the typical student's 5.5-ish average, signaling to the model that your later academic performance placed you in the lower ranks compared to peers who generally pass the bar. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile of 2, again lower than the average around 5.7, reinforcing concerns about consistently weaker grades relative to other students. Your upper-middle family income also contributed toward the failure prediction, as the model associates certain income brackets with outcomes less favorable than those seen in the broader dataset. While your LSAT and GPA were somewhat below averages too, and full-time status is common among admitted students, they played smaller roles here. In contrast to typical students—who often have mid-range deciles, higher test scores, and profiles aligned with bar passers—your early and later school rankings stood out as the biggest drags, making your overall profile riskier in the model's eyes. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background, but a data-driven flag based on historical trends. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score above the 36.7 average, strengthening your academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program to improve those decile rankings, and gathering strong letters of recommendation that highlight your resilience as a Hispanic male pursuing law. Reapplying after addressing these gaps could shift the prediction in your favor, and many students in similar situations have succeeded by targeting community colleges or targeted prep first. You're taking a brave step into law, and with focused improvements, doors can reopen.
